items.title: GINA severity classification
items.contentHtml:
GINA classification of severity

  • The Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) classifies severity into intermittent, mild persistent, moderate persistent and severe persistent according to frequency of symptoms, nocturnal awakenings, use of short-acting beta2 agonists and limitation of activity.
  • Intermittent asthma: symptoms 2 days/week, awakenings <2/month, forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1) ≥80% of predicted.
  • Mild persistent asthma: symptoms >2 but
  • Moderate persistent asthma: daily symptoms, awakenings >1/week (not daily), FEV1 60-80%.
  • Severe persistent asthma: daily symptoms, frequent nocturnal awakenings, FEV1 <60%.

items.title: Triad of typical symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Triad of typical symptoms

  • The classic triad of asthma includes dyspnea, cough (sometimes as the only symptom, especially at night) and wheezing.
  • chest tightness is a common symptom but is not part of the classic triad; It may accompany dyspnea.
  • The cough is usually non-productive or with little mucoid sputum; in exacerbations there may be purulent sputum if there is infection.
  • Symptoms are variable over time, they worsen with exercise, allergens, cold air or emotions.
  • On examination, expiratory wheezing is the most characteristic finding; Its absence does not rule out asthma (e.g., very severe crisis with silent chest).

items.title: Differential diagnosis with COPD
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with COPD

  • Asthma usually begins in childhood/adolescence, while chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) appears in >40 years with smoking.
  • reversibility is characteristic of asthma (positive bronchodilator test with increased forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1) ≥12% and ≥200 ml); in COPD the reversibility is lower.
  • In asthma, the variability of peak expiratory flow is >20% in the day; in COPD the diurnal variability is <10%.
  • The response to inhaled corticosteroids is excellent in asthma; in COPD, although useful, the response is less marked.
  • The diffusion of carbon monoxide (DLCO) is normal or increased in asthma, while in COPD it is usually reduced.

items.title: positive bronchodilator test
items.contentHtml:
Positive bronchodilator test

  • It is performed with salbutamol (400 mcg in a metered dose inhaler or 2.5 mg nebulized) by measuring the forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1) before and 15-20 minutes later.
  • A positive bronchodilator test is considered when there is an increase in FEV1 ≥12% and ≥200 ml compared to the baseline value.
  • A positive response indicates reversibility of the obstruction and supports the diagnosis of asthma.
  • The absence of response does not rule out asthma, especially if the patient is asymptomatic or on basic treatment.
  • In patients with very low forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1), the test can be unreliable; It is recommended to repeat it another time.

items.title: Peak expiratory flow and variability
items.contentHtml:
Peak expiratory flow and variability

  • The peak expiratory flow (PFE) measures the maximum rate of forced expiration; It is used to monitor diurnal variability.
  • It is measured with a peak flow meter in the morning upon awakening and at night; variability is calculated as (maximum value - minimum value) / maximum value × 100.
  • A diurnal variability >20% (in adults) is indicative of uncontrolled asthma; values ​​>30% suggest imminent exacerbation.
  • In children, a variability >13% is considered positive.
  • Personal peak expiratory flow (PFE) (patient's best value) is used as a reference; falls >20% of the best value indicate worsening.

items.title: Step-by-step treatment
items.contentHtml:
Step therapy

  • The Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) step therapy is based on symptoms and risk of exacerbations; includes 5 steps.
  • Step 1: short-acting beta2 agonists as needed (only if symptoms <2/week, without nocturnal awakenings).
  • Step 2: low-dose inhaled corticosteroids + short-acting beta2 agonists as needed.
  • Step 3: low-dose inhaled corticosteroids + long-acting beta2 agonists. (LABA) (e.g., formoterol) as maintenance and relief therapy.
  • Step 4: medium/high-dose inhaled corticosteroids + long-acting beta2 agonists (LABA).
  • Step 5: add long-acting anticholinergics (tiotropium), biologics (omalizumab, mepolizumab), or high-dose systemic corticosteroids low.

items.title: Use of inhaled corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Use of inhaled corticosteroids

  • Inhaled corticosteroids are the basis of maintenance treatment in persistent asthma; reduce eosinophilic inflammation.
  • Low doses: budesonide 200-400 mcg/day, fluticasone 100-250 mcg/day, beclomethasone 200-400 mcg/day.
  • Medium doses: budesonide 400-800 mcg/day; fluticasone 250-500 mcg/day; beclomethasone 400-800 mcg/day.
  • High doses: budesonide >800 mcg/day; fluticasone >500 mcg/day; beclomethasone >800 mcg/day.
  • Local adverse effects (oropharyngeal candidiasis, dysphonia) are prevented with mouthwash after inhalation.
  • They are not recommended as monotherapy in acute crisis; Its onset of action is slow (days-weeks).

items.title: Short-acting beta2 agonists
items.contentHtml:
Short-acting beta2 agonists

  • Short-acting beta2 agonists (SABA) are the rescue treatment of choice in asthma attacks; the most used is salbutamol (100-200 mcg per inhalation).
  • In acute crisis: 4-8 puffs every 20 minutes during the first hour, then according to response.
  • Nebulization with salbutamol 2.5-5 mg every 20 minutes is an alternative in severe crises.
  • Excessive use (>2 inhalers/year or >8 puffs/day) is associated with a higher risk of exacerbations and mortality.
  • Adverse effects include tachycardia, tremor, hypokalemia; caution in ischemic heart disease.
  • They should not be used as maintenance monotherapy; indicate insufficient control if required >2 times/week.

items.title: Indications for systemic corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Indications for systemic corticosteroids

  • Systemic corticosteroids are indicated in moderate-severe asthma attacks that do not respond to bronchodilators, and in severe persistent asthma of step 5.
  • Oral route: prednisone 40-60 mg/day (or equivalent) for 5-7 days in adults; It does not require gradual tapering if the treatment is short.
  • Intravenous route: hydrocortisone 200 mg or methylprednisolone 60-125 mg every 6-8 hours in very severe crises or if the patient does not tolerate oral administration.
  • In children: prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day (maximum 40 mg/day) for 3-5 days.
  • Systemic maintenance corticosteroids are reserved for asthma refractory to other therapies; use the minimum effective dose and monitor adverse effects (osteoporosis, diabetes, adrenal suppression).

items.title: Asthmatic crisis: classification
items.contentHtml:
Asthmatic crisis: classification

  • Asthmatic crisis is classified as mild, moderate, severe and imminent arrest according to dyspnea, respiratory rate, use of accessory muscles, oxygen saturation (SpO2) and forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1)/peak expiratory flow (PFE).
  • Mild: Dyspnea when walking, complete speech, respiratory rate <20, SpO2 ≥95%, PFE >70% of best.
  • Moderate: Dyspnea when speaking, choppy sentences, respiratory rate 20-30, SpO2 91-95%, PFE 50-70%.
  • Severe: dyspnea at rest, slurred speech, respiratory rate >30, SpO2 <91%, PFE <50%.
  • Imminent arrest: silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, hypotension, SpO2 <90% despite oxygen.

items.title: Signs of severity in crisis
items.contentHtml:
Signs of severity in an attack

  • The signs of severity in an asthma attack include: use of accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, intercostals), suprasternal indrawing and intercostal retraction.
  • Silent chest: absence of wheezing due to severe hypoventilation, indicating critical obstruction and risk of respiratory arrest.
  • Altered state of consciousness (drowsiness, extreme agitation) due to hypoxia or hypercapnia.
  • Heart rate >120 bpm (adults) or >130 bpm (children), pulsus paradoxus >25 mmHg (systolic pressure drop in inspiration).
  • Central cyanosis, oxygen saturation (SpO2) <90% and partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) <60 mmHg with room air.

items.foco: Acute management with oxygen and bronchodilators
items.title: Acute management with oxygen and bronchodilators
items.contentHtml:
Acute management with oxygen and bronchodilators

  • Initiate supplementary oxygen to maintain oxygen saturation (SpO2) ≥92-95% (adults) or ≥94-98% (children); use a reservoir mask at 10-15 L/min if necessary.
  • Administer nebulized salbutamol (2.5-5 mg) or 4-8 puffs with a spacer chamber every 20 minutes during the first hour; then space according to response.
  • Add ipratropium bromide (0.5 mg nebulized or 4-8 puffs) in moderate-severe crisis; reduces hospitalization.
  • In severe or unresponsive crisis, consider intravenous magnesium sulfate (2 g in 20 minutes) as an additional bronchodilator.
  • Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60 mg orally or hydrocortisone 200 mg intravenously) should be administered early in moderate-severe crisis.
  • If there are signs of arrest imminent, prepare for orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation.
